title: POMDP-based Object Search with Growing State Space and Hybrid Action Domain
abstract: 在复杂室内环境中，面对书架、桌子、床等多样家具，移动机器人高效定位目标物体是一项重大挑战。该难题源于定位误差、视野受限以及视觉遮挡等因素。我们将物体搜索任务建模为三维环境下具有增长状态空间与混合（连续/离散）动作空间的高维部分可观测马尔可夫决策过程（POMDP）。基于精心设计的感知模块，本文提出一种名为增长神经过程滤波k中心聚类树（GNPF-kCT）的新型在线POMDP求解器。该求解器通过蒙特卡洛树搜索（MCTS）选择最优动作，并采用以下机制：针对增长状态空间的信念树复用技术、用于过滤无效原始动作的神经过程网络，以及通过k中心聚类超球面离散化实现高维动作空间的高效精细化搜索。改进的上置信界（UCB）算法结合状态估计直径内单元格的信念差异与动作价值函数，引导MCTS扩展过程。理论分析验证了方法的收敛性与性能潜力。针对信息或奖励匮乏的场景，我们引入基于网格世界模型的猜想目标物体策略，将其作为提升搜索效率的关键手段。在Gazebo仿真环境中，与基于POMDP的基线方法及最新非POMDP求解器（尤其是大语言模型方法）相比，搭载Fetch与Stretch机器人的大量实验表明，在相同计算约束与感知系统下，本方法能实现更快速、更可靠的目标定位。办公室环境中的实际部署测试进一步证实了方法的工程实用性。项目页面：https://sites.google.com/view/gnpfkct

POMDP-based object search with growing state space and hybrid action domain
Yongbo Chen

Abstract
Efficiently locating target objects in complex indoor environments with diverse furniture, such as shelves, tables, and beds, is a significant challenge for mobile robots. This difficulty arises from factors like localization errors, limited fields of view, and visual occlusion. We address this by framing the object-search task as a high-dimensional Partially Observable Markov Decision Process (POMDP) with a growing state space and hybrid (continuous and discrete) action spaces in 3D environments. Based on a meticulously designed perception module, a novel online POMDP solver named the growing neural process filtered
k
k
-center clustering tree (GNPF-
k
k
CT) is proposed to tackle this problem. Optimal actions are selected using Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) with belief tree reuse for growing state space, a neural process network to filter useless primitive actions, and
k
k
-center clustering hypersphere discretization for efficient refinement of high-dimensional action spaces. A modified upper-confidence bound (UCB), informed by belief differences and action value functions within cells of estimated diameters, guides MCTS expansion. Theoretical analysis validates the convergence and performance potential of our method. To address scenarios with limited information or rewards, we also introduce a guessed target object with a grid-world model as a key strategy to enhance search efficiency. Extensive Gazebo simulations with Fetch and Stretch robots demonstrate faster and more reliable target localization than POMDP-based baselines and state-of-the-art (SOTA) non-POMDP-based solvers, especially large language model (LLM) based methods, in object search under the same computational constraints and perception systems. Real-world tests in office environments confirm the practical applicability of our approach. Project page:
https://sites.google.com/view/gnpfkct
.
keywords:
Object-searching, partially observable Markov decision process (POMDP), Monte Carlo tree search (MCTS),
k
k
-center clustering tree, neural process (NP), and belief tree reuse.
1
Introduction
Object search in partially familiar, cluttered environments is crucial for mobile robots. For example, a robot retrieving a customer’s order from a warehouse or fetching a Coke bottle from a kitchen must often locate a target object that is partially or fully obscured by other items. Known as the ‘Mechanical Search’ problem
R1 (
Danielczuk et al., 2003
)
, it typically involves leveraging partial environmental knowledge, like maps of large furniture (e.g., shelves, tables), while smaller objects (e.g., mugs, glasses) remain unlocalized due to frequent movement. Humans typically find smaller objects by relying on prior knowledge, assuming familiarity with the target, and removing obstructions as needed
aydemir2013active (
Aydemir et al., 2013
)
. Similarly, robots must adjust their configurations, strategically observe from multiple viewpoints, and interact with the environment by removing obstructions and retrieving the target object. This paper focuses on object search in a multi-room home environment with predefined workspaces. Equipped with 3D point cloud and 2D occupancy grid maps and provided with target photos, the robot aims to efficiently locate objects using onboard sensors, as shown in Fig.
1
.
Figure 1
:
A scenario of object search involves a Fetch robot locating and removing a pink snack box from a table using feature matching. The robot actively adjusts its base, lift, and head (red) to change its view, with no prior knowledge of the size, number, or color of objects on the table.
The object search method depends on a comprehensive support system that includes modules for object segmentation, object detection, pose estimation, task-level planning, and action-level planning. Significant advancements have been made in these supporting areas, particularly through advanced learning technologies. For example, an impressive approach presented in
R3 (
Shaban et al., 2017
)
showcases an efficient one-shot learning method utilizing a fully convolutional network for pixel-level image segmentation. Similarly, other methods leverage architectures like neural radiance fields and convolutional neural networks (CNNs) to address challenges in optimal grasp pose generation
R4 (
Sóti et al., 2023
)
and manipulation planning
R5 (
Qureshi et al., 2020
)
. In this study, we focus on task-level planning, aiming to select primitive actions and achieve long-term sequential goals for locating and retrieving target objects in complex, unlabeled environments.
In this paper, we propose a partially observable Markov decision process (POMDP) formulation with a hybrid action domain for object search, integrating well-defined perception and action modules. To solve this formulation, we introduce a novel solver that leverages belief tree reuse, neural process filtering, and
k
k
-center clustering. The main contributions of this work are as follows:
•
Realistic formulation
: A complete POMDP formulation for object search task considering a realistic perception process.
•
Better exploration trick
: Introduction of the guessed target object with odds update for better exploration.
•
Novel efficient POMDP solver
: A novel solver for POMDP problem with growing state space, neural process filtering, and hybrid (continuous and discrete) action domain.
•
Demo
: Realistic gazebo simulations and real-world demos using two robots, showcasing the proposed framework with publicly available open-source code.
This paper extends our previous work
chen2024pomdp (
Chen et al., 2024a
)
and an on-line document
anonymous2024npfkct (
Chen et al., 2024b
)
. The paper is organized as follows: Section
2
reviews related work on POMDP models, existing solvers, and mechanical search. Section
3
outlines our problem formulation and solvers for the POMDP-based object search task. Section
4
details the POMDP formulation, including state, action, and observation spaces, and key functions.
Section
5
introduces a novel online POMDP solver with theoretical discussions. In Section
6
, we explain our action execution and perception parts using the Fetch robot with onboard sensors to support our POMDP formulation. Section
7
provides simulation and experimental results validating the practicality and high performance of the proposed method. Finally, we summarize our conclusions and present our future work in Section
8
.
2
Related work
2.1
Mechanical search: Planning
Robots frequently encounter challenges navigating cluttered environments to locate target objects. Many contemporary approaches leverage advancements in learning technologies, including deep reinforcement learning (RL)
kurenkov2020visuomotor (
Kurenkov et al., 2020
)
, deep Q-learning
yang2020deep (
Yang et al., 2020
)
, and deep-geometric inference systems
huang2021mechanical (
Huang et al., 2021
)
. For instance,
kurenkov2020visuomotor (
Kurenkov et al., 2020
)
proposes an innovative learning procedure that combines an asymmetric architecture to exploit privileged information, guidance from suboptimal teachers, and mid-level representations for training deep RL agents to discover target objects hidden within piles of unknown items. However, these methods are tailored to specific environments, such as shelves or boxes, limiting their applicability in diverse, unstructured settings.
Beyond learning-based methods, the POMDP framework offers a compact and effective approach for object search. As an example, in
huang2022mechanical (
Huang et al., 2022
)
, a Monte-Carlo tree search method featuring a 1D occupancy distribution for objects is employed to swiftly identify and extract the target object. Due to its natural compatibility with scenarios involving multiple detected objects, the object-oriented POMDP (OO-POMDP) framework is widely adopted. This framework factors objects into classes with associated attributes, treating the beliefs for different objects as independent, which enables linear scaling of belief size and reduces computational costs
wandzel2019multi (
Wandzel et al., 2019
)
. To simplify computation, prior work often restricts the workspace to a 2D plane with simple transition and observation models. For example,
aydemir2013active (
Aydemir et al., 2013
)
proposed a 2D POMDP formulation for object search in a room by evaluating candidate viewpoints. Recent efforts have extended this to 3D environments with object-size-friendly properties using multi-resolution planning algorithms
zheng2021multi (
Zheng et al., 2021
)
. The commonly used POMDP solver in these frameworks is partially observable Monte Carlo planning (POMCP)
silver2010monte (
Silver et al., 2010
)
. We build on the OO-POMDP framework for real-world applicability, but go forward one step to consider the belief relationship between different objects in a complicated 3D environment including some challenging cases with partially and fully occluded objects. We also formulate the object-level POMDP formulation with a growing state space, a hybrid action domain, and a guessed target object.
2.2
Mechanical search: Reasoning
Earlier robotics approaches integrated reasoning directly into the planning process, such as updating probabilities, rather than treating it as a distinct module. As robotic systems became more sophisticated, reasoning evolved into a separate layer, addressing high-level cognitive tasks like commonsense inference, contextual understanding, and hypothesis generation. This reasoning layer then informs a planning module to execute detailed action strategies. For instance,
giuliari2023leveraging (
Giuliari et al., 2023
)
demonstrates a reasoning module that uses environmental context and commonsense knowledge to infer plausible object locations, aiding localization in partially observed scenes. Similarly,
ge2024commonsense (
Ge et al., 2024
)
employs commonsense knowledge from large language models to construct scene graphs for improving object search. However, these methods struggle with unconventional object arrangements, such as randomized setups. In our scenario, objects are deliberately placed in configurations that defy typical human habits, increasing search complexity and requiring active robot-environment interactions, such as occlusion removal. Our reasoning idea is based on the probability update of the grid world. By representing the belief over the pose state of each object in the planning environment using particle filtering, the authors in
garrett2020online (
Garrett et al., 2020
)
incorporate probabilistic reasoning into a deterministic planner and then perform replanning when facing the base movement failure. Recent work
huang2024out (
Huang et al., 2024
)
utilizes a video tracking-based memory model with integrated reasoning and planning capabilities. This allows the system to remember potential locations of occluded target objects and complete tasks using appropriate actions.
2.3
POMDP formulation and solvers
For a POMDP agent, the process commences with a brief, where the agent deduces and executes the optimal action to transition to a new state. This new state is concealed within an updated belief, refined through Bayesian inference based on observed data. Each step yields an immediate reward and contributes to a discounted cumulative reward, fostering a long-term objective. This iterative process continues until terminal conditions are met. The POMDP framework is a powerful tool for planning under uncertainty, excelling in non-deterministic and partially observable scenarios. Widely applied in robotics, it addresses tasks like autonomous underwater vehicle navigation
Hou_belief (
Hou et al., 2021
)
, robot manipulation
pajarinen2022pomdp (
Pajarinen et al., 2019
)
, and Human-Robot collaboration
burks2023harps (
Burks et al., 2023
)
. Our work adopts this approach, developing an object-oriented POMDP model with hybrid action domains.
Finding the exact optimal strategy for a POMDP problem is widely recognized as computationally intractable
papadimitriou1987complexity (
Papadimitriou et al., 1987
)
. Over the past two decades, POMDP solvers have been categorized into offline and online methods. Offline solvers, such as point-based POMDP methods
Shani_2013 (
Shani et al., 2013
,
Kurniawati et al., 2009
)
, can produce optimal solutions for small-scale problems. However, their significant memory requirements often make them impractical for use on standard computational platforms in real-world applications. In contrast, online solvers, especially sampling-based methods, offer a practical trade-off by achieving approximate optimality with greater computational efficiency, making them suitable for various applications.
Sampling-based solvers, such as POMCP
silver2010monte (
Silver et al., 2010
)
, adaptive belief tree (ABT)
kurniawati2016online (
Kurniawati et al., 2016
)
, and determinized sparse partially observable tree (DESPOT)
somani2013despot (
Somani et al., 2013
)
, share a common strategy: representing belief as particles and utilizing Monte Carlo tree search (MCTS) to expand the belief tree within computational limits. Despite significant advancements, solving POMDPs with high-dimensional continuous action spaces remains a substantial challenge. The key point in existing continuous-action POMDPs is to refine the action subset incrementally to improve the possibility that the selected subset of actions contains the best action. Methods like partially observable Monte Carlo planning with observation widening (POMCPOW)
sunberg2018online (
Sunberg et al., 2018
)
use Progressive Widening to add new sampled actions after sufficient exploration, while others integrate techniques like Voronoi optimistic optimization
Michael2021Voronoi (
Lim et al., 2021
)
or Bayesian optimization
mern2021bayesian (
Mern et al., 2021
)
. These methods commonly apply the UCB1
auer2002finite (
Auer et al., 2002
)
algorithm for exploration and Monte Carlo backups for value estimation, differing mainly in how they refine the action domain. Despite advances, the challenges posed by growing state spaces and continuous state, action, and observation spaces remain formidable.
2.4
Neural process in robotics
As a more efficient alternative to Gaussian processes, neural processes excel in function regression by capturing uncertainty in predictions as a stochastic process. In numerous robotics tasks, the ability to account for uncertainty in predictions is crucial for enhancing system robustness and practicality. For instance,
chen2022meta (
Chen et al., 2022
)
introduces a conditional neural process (CNP)-based meta-learning algorithm to estimate grasp points from limited depth image trials. The versatility of CNPs is further demonstrated in applications such as 6D pose estimation
Li2022Category (
Li et al., 2022
)
and understanding social aspects of navigation
yildirim2022learning (
Yildirim et al., 2022
)
. In our work, we utilize neural processes to address uncertain scoring function regression and primitive action filtering, creating a robust framework for decision-making in complex, dynamic environments. This idea was publicly released in our earlier work
anonymous2024npfkct (
Chen et al., 2024b
)
, preceding several concurrent studies exploring neural process-based planning
Jain2025Learning (
Jain et al., 2025
)
.
3
Overall framework
In a cluttered indoor environment with numerous unknown movable and immovable obstacles, a robot must locate and pick up a movable target object using onboard sensors and reference photos. To solve this, we use a POMDP-based approach, GNPF-
k
k
CT, with 4 alternating stages: planning, execution, observation, and filtering. In the planning stage, a look-ahead MCTS exploration uses an initial belief, transition, and reward functions, observation model, rollout strategy, and terminal function to sample particles and determine the best action. To address challenges from a growing state space and continuous action domain, we enhance the POMDP solver with belief tree reuse, neural process filtering, and
k
k
-center clustering, improving efficiency and performance.
At the execution and observation stages, the algorithm performs the selected action on the robot, which gathers real-world measurements from onboard sensors, including 2D lidar scans, 3D point clouds, and RGBD camera images, without relying on markers like QR codes. These measurements are processed into state-space information through steps like robot pose estimation, point cloud filtering and segmentation, principal component analysis, object matching, and move-ability estimation. In the filtering stage, a particle filter updates the belief and prunes the belief tree using processed sensor data. The guessed target object and its grid world are updated via log-odds, similar to occupancy grid mapping. Robot pose estimation uses iterative closest point (ICP), initialized by adaptive Monte Carlo localization (AMCL). If the terminal condition is met, the task ends with the cumulative reward; otherwise, the updated belief reuses the existing belief tree and resamples particles to proceed with the MCTS. These steps, as shown in Fig.
2
, collectively achieve object search and retrieval.
Figure 2
:
The main steps in our proposed approach, GNPF-
k
k
CT

4.2
Focused object search problem
Our object search task involves locating a movable target object in clustered workspaces with unknown numbers (
n
>
1
n>1
) of obstacles (e.g., cups, laptops, or books) at unknown locations. We also have the point cloud and occupancy grid maps for a planning environment with furniture for robot navigation. While point clouds for some known obstacle objects are available, the target object and many other obstacles lack prior point cloud data, requiring detection via onboard sensors during execution. Unlike the assumption in OO-POMDP, because we measure the information about the object using real-world sensors, the object beliefs in our scenario are not independent and may have effects on each other. Additionally, the planning environment is continuous, with parameters such as object poses represented as continuous variables rather than discrete ones.

Reward function
R
R
.
The reward function is a crucial component of the POMDP formulation, guiding the agent toward successfully completing the task. The robot’s expected sequence of actions is that the robot begins by moving to the workspace for initial observation, iteratively adjusts its viewpoint to update log-odds in object grids, declares objects as obstacles or targets, and finally removes obstacles to improve visibility or directly removes the target to complete the task. Gaussian Processes (GPs) are a natural choice for modeling such functions, leveraging probabilistic inference to update predictions, but they become computationally expensive in high-dimensional tasks. To address this, we propose to use Neural Processes (NPs), which also define distributions over functions while being computationally efficient and adaptable to various inputs leveraging the advantages of neural networks. Unlike traditional approaches like Q-learning
hausknecht2015deep (

, we adopt a simpler scoring network. This network prioritizes short-term, physically meaningful goals (e.g., single-step objectives), making training and implementation more practical. The NP-based scoring network efficiently filters out ineffective actions, enabling a more compact POMDP framework to select optimal actions based on the filtered actions and current beliefs. of all nodes visited by this episode. Here, we present two classical stochastic backup methods including the Bellman backup (Algorithm
6
), which is used
in the ABT method and similar to the rule used in Q-learning, and the Monte-Carlo backup (Algorithm
6
), which is widely used in many outstanding POMDP solvers, like POMCP, POMCPOW, and Voronoi Optimistic Monte Carlo Planning with Observation Weighting (VOMCPOW)
Michael2021Voronoi (
Lim et al., 2021
)
. The Bellman update naturally follows the objective function of the POMDP formulation that aims to pursue optimal action in each step of the long-term planning. It helps the solver to explore deeper by focusing its search on promising parts of the belief tree. The main challenge for the Bellman backup is when facing unexpected observations, a lot of deeply explored belief trees will be frequently cut and this case causes poor planning performance. Hence, the Bellman backup gets a better performance when the good rewards are sparse in the belief tree, but it is not stable enough for the poor observation prediction. In contrast to selecting the reward with optimal action, the Monte-Carlo backup computes the average reward along with different action episodes, which means that the generated belief tree will be more robust when facing unexpected uncertainty in received observation. In our application for object search, the real visual observation is not well predictable for the observation model in the POMDP formulation and the robot camera will frequently receive unexpected measurements, which breaks the advantage of the Bellman update. We use the Monte-Carlo backup in our problem but the Bellman backup is commonly superior in other applications, so we present both two backup ways here.

Action execution and perception
Unlike many state-of-the-art methods
zheng2021multi (
Zheng et al., 2021
,
Zheng et al., 2022
)
that assume static objects and no robot-object interactions, our approach enables the robotic arm to actively clear obstacles, uncovering hidden spaces. Our perception module combines point cloud and image data to estimate object poses, sizes, movability, and detection, eliminating the need for manual markers like QR codes.
6.1
Action execution
Our object search framework supports mobile robots with 2D LiDAR and RGB-D cameras, focusing on the Fetch robot. Navigation relies on the ROS move_base interface with an AMCL-based stack
Quigley2009ROS (
Quigley et al., 2009
)
. Lift height and head joints are controlled via ros_control
Chitta2017ros (
Chitta et al., 2017
)
, while object removal uses moveit
Sucan2012The (
Sucan et al., 2012
)
to pick and place items outside the workspace. The basic actions are shown in Fig.
8
.

6.3
Object detector
State-of-the-art real-time object detection systems like YOLO classify objects into predefined categories, which may not match specific targets. Meanwhile, 3D point clouds provide valuable data for image segmentation. To tackle object detection with limited images and optional semantic cues, we combine traditional feature matching with YOLO, leveraging their strengths for accurate and efficient detection.
Building on point cloud segmentation, we project segmented local frame point clouds onto the image using the camera configuration, creating sub-images. YOLOv5

)
generates sub-images with semantic scores

7
Simulations and real-world experiments
7.1
Simulations
We validate our approach using Fetch and Stretch robot simulators in the Gazebo environment, implemented by C++ and Python. The neural process network is trained for 3000 iterations (approximately 4 hours) on a single NVIDIA 3090 GPU. Post-training, we execute our project on a desktop machine, utilizing only the CPU, operating on Ubuntu 18.04, and powered by an Intel Core i7-13700k processor. Our evaluation spans diverse object configurations and scenarios, comparing our method against benchmarks, including continuous action domain benchmark methods (POMCPOW and VOMCPOW) and classical POMDP methods with manual-setting discrete action domain (POMCP, GPOMCP
chen2024pomdp (
Chen et al., 2024a
)
, and NPF-
k
k
CT, which is sub-version of our proposed GNPF-
k
k
CT method without belief tree reuse
anonymous2024npfkct (
Chen et al., 2024b
)
.).
7.1.1

The simulations occur in a realistic Gazebo living environment with diverse object configurations. Navigation relies on a point cloud map and a 2D occupancy grid, featuring furniture and some predefined objects, while most objects are unknown. Large furniture surfaces serve as workspaces, with a target object (a blue snack box) and various obstacles placed randomly. Methods are evaluated across five scenarios, with robot candidate positions defined by four red rectangles around each workspace, unrestricted in orientation. Many existing object search methods rely on classical POMCP, which operates in a discrete action domain. We evaluate our method against both discrete-action POMDP solvers (POMCP, GPOMCP) and continuous-action solvers (POMCPOW, VOMCPOW, NPF-
k
k
CT). For POMCP and GPOMCP, robot configurations
𝒂
r
\bm{a}_{r}
are manually limited to combinations of 4 selected poses, 3 lift heights, and 9 head orientations, reducing coverage of the continuous action domain. Although this creates an unfair comparison, it highlights the benefits of broader continuous action domains. All methods address object search with a guessed target object and are tested across five scenarios of varying complexity: Loose1 (4 objects, 1 workspace), Loose2 (6 objects, 2 workspaces), Hidden1 (7 objects, 1 workspace), Covered1 (7 objects, 1 workspace), and Complex1 (15 objects, 1 workspace), as shown in Fig.
14
. The Covered1 and Complex1 scenarios are particularly challenging, as the robot is unable to observe the target object from most configurations, even when operating at the maximum lift height. Several representative cases illustrating occluded observations, even under favorable robot configurations, are shown in Fig.

. Each scenario involves 20 trials, and results, including the

Our method and NPF-
k
k
CT exhibit a significant advantage across all scenarios due to the efficient NP filtering and robust clustering refinement. These mechanisms optimize robot configurations—poses, lift heights, and head orientations—for effective object observation. The continuous action refinement further facilitates the discovery of rare and challenging configurations, which are typically difficult to pinpoint in a large continuous action domain. This capability allows the robot to effectively locate and observe target objects, as illustrated in Fig.
16
.

Good observations based on tricky robot configurations (very common to obtain based on our method).
In simpler scenarios like Loose1 and Hidden1, NP filtering and refined clustering enable the robot’s FOV to quickly cover the workspace, often observing all objects in the first step. In these cases, belief tree reuse is less effective or counterproductive due to added complexity (Section
5.4
), no new observations, and the more predictable measurements within the belief tree. However, belief tree reuse becomes advantageous in complex scenarios like Complex1 and Covered1, where the robot encounters numerous unexpected objects during the search process.

7.3
Comparison with non-POMDP framework
All previous results were obtained using our proposed POMDP framework. In this section, we compare our method with several representative non-POMDP planning frameworks, including Random, NPF-G, SGoLAM
KimJ (
Kim et al., 2021
)
, SayPlan
Rana1953Sayplan (
Rana et al., 2023
)
, and MoMa-LLM
HonerkampHonerkamp (
Honerkamp et al., 2024
)
. Since these planning methods differ substantially in their original problem formulations and configuration settings, we adapt them to our task by minimally revising their action spaces and unifying the perception module across all methods, ensuring a fair comparison. We first provide a qualitative summary of the original capabilities of these methods, as reported in the corresponding references, in Table
4
.

As shown in Table
4
, our framework is implemented in the high-fidelity Gazebo environment and operates with minimal prior information about the scene. The entire pipeline, including perception and manipulation actions, is designed to be directly transferable to real robotic systems with very little modification. In contrast, LLM-based methods such as SayPlan and MoMa-LLM typically rely on pre-given semantic annotations or simulator-provided ground-truth information. For example, MoMa-LLM directly accesses pixel-level semantic perception from the iGibson simulator and employs QR-code markers in real-world experiments, both of which are treated as uncertainty-free prior knowledge (marked as Pre-given in Table
4
). These simplified assumptions make certain actions easier, for instance, exploration can be performed by a single rotation that immediately reveals all object labels. Moreover, SGoLAM and SayPlan do not support interactive actions that modify the environment configuration. While MoMa-LLM includes limited interactions such as opening doors or refrigerators, it does not allow interactive manipulation of detected objects. In contrast, our framework explicitly models environment interaction and uncertainty, enabling more realistic and transferable decision-making. We next detail the compared methods:

SGoLAM
KimJ (
Kim et al., 2021

achieved top performance in the CVPR 2021 MultiON Challenge using a purely non-learning-based strategy. The method first conducts frontier-based exploration to search the environment and locate the target object, and then switches to a dedicated planner for efficient navigation. To ensure a fair comparison with our framework, which explicitly provides the workspace area, we reduce the frontier exploration step size and perform exploration directly in the continuous position domain (highlighted by red rectangles in Fig.
19
). Without this modification, SGoLAM exhibits significantly degraded performance in our setting. Moreover, since the continuous exploration positions are already close to the objects, no additional navigation step is required to further increase the target object’s pixel size after frontier exploration. For perception, we use the same configuration as our method. At each frontier point, exploration is performed by selecting the three most distinctive orientations from 100 uniformly sampled candidates.

To better fit our task setting, we extend the original action set by introducing additional primitive actions, including object declaration and removal of declared objects. Most of these methods are developed and evaluated in large-scale environments using simulators such as iGibson and Habitat. When applied to our single-room setting, they exhibit substantially degraded performance. This motivates a discussion of several common misconceptions in the literature, which we summarize as follows:
Question 5
.
Is a single-room environment with clustered objects inherently easier for mobile robots than multi-room, multi-level environments with sparse objects? Are existing LLM-based methods currently superior to model-based approaches?
Discussion 2
.
Our answers to both questions are
no
, as demonstrated by the experimental results presented earlier.
A key reason is that the field of view (FOV) of common mobile robots becomes severely limited when operating close to objects, making visual blind spots frequent and unavoidable. At the same time, operating at longer distances is unfavorable for reliable object matching and precise manipulation, necessitating close-range interactions. Many existing works adopt simplified assumptions, for example, treating the object search task as complete once the target object enters a predefined sensing range, which are often unrealistic in practice. Such approaches also fail to fully exploit changes in the robot’s configuration, such as head orientation or lift height, thereby limiting their effectiveness in realistic object search scenarios, particularly when objects are densely clustered.
Another contributing factor to the inferior performance of LLM-based methods in our setting is their limited capacity to faithfully represent physical constraints and spatial uncertainty. Natural language abstractions are inherently ambiguous and lack the precision required for fine-grained robotic execution. Importantly, object search in a cluttered single-room environment constitutes a physically grounded task involving perception, manipulation, and configuration changes, rather than a purely topological navigation problem commonly studied in large-scale, sparsely populated environments.
Furthermore, we observe that for LLM-based methods, it remains challenging to design prompts that guarantee not only the feasibility but also the optimality of both the problem formulation and the resulting solution. Most existing studies primarily focus on whether a task can be completed, with limited analysis of optimal decision-making under uncertainty. This limitation is closely related to the absence of an explicit internal world model in current LLMs, which hinders long-horizon action–outcome simulation.
Nevertheless, our results indicate a clear improvement in long-horizon planning performance from GPT-3.5 to GPT-5 (Table
5
), suggesting that progress in this direction is ongoing. Overall, we believe that integrating model-based reasoning tailored to single-room physical interactions with learning-based global reasoning mechanisms represents a more promising research direction, and is likely to lead to more realistic and robust solutions for real-world object search tasks.
7.4
Limitation
Our approach faces several limitations. First, reliance on a pre-existing map is impractical for real-world robots, but this can be mitigated with advanced SLAM techniques. Second, achieving a 100% success rate for primitive actions in real-world scenarios is unrealistic, affecting overall performance. Additionally, our use of point cloud segmentation may generate incorrect bounding boxes for objects with large contact areas, leading to errors in data association and belief updates. Object detection methods like YOLO, SIFT, and color matching also face challenges in environments with limited features, low coverage, or poor lighting. But we believe these issues can be addressed through advances in perception, navigation, and execution, which are outside the scope of this paper.
8
Conclusion and future work
This paper introduces GNPF-
k
k
CT, a novel POMDP-based framework and solver for object search tasks in a 3D environment using a mobile robot manipulator. The problem is modeled as an 8-tuple POMDP, focusing on FOV adjustments and belief updates for observed objects. By incorporating a guessed target object, our approach efficiently locates hidden objects by removing obstacles, a limitation in existing methods. Our framework combines a pre-trained NP network to select potential primitive actions from Gazebo simulation data with adaptive
k
k
-clustering hypersphere partitioning to refine the continuous action space for efficient MCTS exploration. This integrates short-term learning with a compact long-term POMDP framework for improved performance. To address challenges from growing state spaces, we reuse the previous belief tree and apply a revised UCB1 bound, incorporating hypersphere range and belief distance for efficient and robust decision-making. Experiments show that, our solver surpasses classical methods in cumulative reward, task steps, and success rate under identical computational constraints. Notably, our framework requires only maps, photos, and onboard sensors, eliminating the need for geometric models or additional object-specific data, and making it highly practical for real-world applications.
This paper lays the groundwork for autonomous object search. Future work will integrate advanced methods, such as LLMs
Zirui2024Large (
Zhao et al., 2024a
)
to embed expert knowledge via prompts
Tang2025Task (
Tang et al., 2025
)
, or world model
Zheng_Yupeng (
Yupeng et al., 2025
)
to fast predict system in latent space, and enable autonomous map creation and workspace generation. More importantly, in experimental results, we find that one of the main limitations is the success rate of the complicated primitive actions. Even though we can consider these uncertainties in the planning framework, they will still cause the low success rate of the whole system with poor preventive implementation. The well-signed Vision-Language-Action (VLA) method, like RT-2
Zitkovich_B (
Zitkovich et al., 2023
)
and Octo
Team_Octo (
Team et al., 2024
)
, may be helpful in completing better primitive actions. These advancements aim to develop fully autonomous, end-to-end systems capable of identifying any target in unknown environments with a single robotic platform and onboard sensors.

Appendix G
LLM prompts example for Sayplan
The high-level LLM prompts example for Sayplan is shown in this section.
LLM prompts example for Sayplan
Content:
You are a Fetch robot in an unexplored house. Your task is to find and remove a blue snack box, its corresponding property is labelled as target
_
\_
object, based on your head camera.
You have the coordinates of the four vertices of each workspace, and all objects are limited to these known workspaces:
Workspace 1: (5.7, 0.61), (5.66, 1.29), (7.5, 1.29), (7.5, 0.61).
You know the move ranges of your pan (radian), tilt (radian), and lift (meter) joints are: (-0.261666667, 0.261666667), (0.0, 0.5), (0.0, 0.4)
You have the following actions available that you can use to achieve this task:
1.
navigate(room_name, object_name): navigate to a configuration (pose and joint angles) that can observe this object in this room.
2.
claim_to_obstacle(room_name, object_name): claim this object is the obstacle object, when the object has a high possibility to be the obstacle object.
3.
claim_to_target(room_name, object_name): claim this object is the target object, when the object has a high possibility to be the obstacle object.
4.
remove(room_name, object_name): remove this target or obstacle object to complete task or free some field of view for better exploration.
5.
explore(room_name): randomly select a frontier to explore the unknown space near one of the rooms that is not fully explored yet.
6.
done(): call when the task (target object is removed) is completed or if you are unable to take any further actions.
Output Response Format:
Analysis:
describe where you could find the objects of interest and what actions you need to execute to get there.
Reasoning:
justify why the next action is important to solve the task.

What is the best next action to complete the task as efficiently as possible? I you don’t think that the object can be found in a known room, prioritize opening doors over exploring a room.
Remember:

Respond with a function call

The explore action allows the robot to adjust its configuration, change its field of view, and make an initial observation of the object. This first observation is essential, as it provides the basis for updating the object and reaching its index.
4.
To observe objects from different directions first to get the belief update until reach obstacle or target index. If the updating object reaches the obstacle or target index, call the claim action first. If it is claimed as a target object (greatly welcome), directly call the remove action and then be done. Task completed and you do not need to navigate to it.
5.
If an object is likely to be the target object, it is good to navigate to this object and observe it from different directions for later claim action.
6.
The claim of both the target and obstacle objects is encouraged. After an object is claimed as the obstacle object, it is legal to remove it to free some field of view. However, the removal action often fails in real-world settings, so obstacle removal should be used cautiously and only attempted occasionally.
7.
If some actions are repeated several times, they may not be possible and you need to observe that object from more directions using explore action.
8.
Each object needs to be claimed as the target or obstacle object first and then it is legal to remove it. Directly remove is illegal.
9.
You can’t explore rooms that haven’t been found yet.

LLM prompts example for MoMa-LLM
Content:
You are a Fetch robot in an unexplored house. Your task is to find and remove a blue snack box, its corresponding property is labelled as target_object, based on your head camera.
You have the coordinates of the four vertices of each workspace and all objects are limited in these known workspaces:
Workspace 1: (5.7, 0.61), (5.66, 1.29), (7.5, 1.29), (7.5, 0.61).
You know the move ranges of your pan (radian), tilt (radian), and lift (meter) joints are: (-0.261666667, 0.261666667), (0.0, 0.5), (0.0, 0.4)
You have the following actions available that you can use to achieve this task:
1.
navigate(room_name, object_name): navigate to a configuration (pose and joint angles) that can observe this object in this room.
2.
claim_to_obstacle(room_name, object_name): claim this object is the obstacle object, when the object has a high possibility to be the obstacle object.
3.
claim_to_target(room_name, object_name): claim this object is the target object, when the object has a high possibility to be the obstacle object.
4.
remove(room_name, object_name): remove this target or obstacle object to complete task or free some field of view for better exploration.
5.
explore(room_name): randomly select a frontier to explore the unknown space near one of the rooms that is not fully explored yet.
6.
done(): call when the task (target object is removed) is completed or if you are unable to take any further actions.
Output Response Format:
Analysis:
describe where you could find the objects of interest and what actions you need to execute to get there.
Reasoning:
justify why the next action is important to solve the task.
Command:
function call

preview_text:
Efficiently locating target objects in complex indoor environments with diverse furniture, such as shelves, tables, and beds, is a significant challenge for mobile robots. This difficulty arises from factors like localization errors, limited fields of view, and visual occlusion. We address this by framing the object-search task as a highdimensional Partially Observable Markov Decision Process (POMDP) with a growing state space and hybrid (continuous and discrete) action spaces in 3D environments. Based on a meticulously designed perception module, a novel online POMDP solver named the growing neural process filtered k-center clustering tree (GNPF-kCT) is proposed to tackle this problem. Optimal actions are selected using Monte Carlo Tree Search (MCTS) with belief tree reuse for growing state space, a neural process network to filter useless primitive actions, and k-center clustering hypersphere discretization for efficient refinement of high-dimensional action spaces. A modified upper-confidence bound (UCB), informed by belief differences and action value functions within cells of estimated diameters, guides MCTS expansion. Theoretical analysis validates the convergence and performance potential of our method. To address scenarios with limited information or rewards, we also introduce a guessed target object with a grid-world model as a key strategy to enhance search efficiency. Extensive Gazebo simulations with Fetch and Stretch robots demonstrate faster and more reliable target localization than POMDP-based baselines and state-of-the-art (SOTA) non-POMDP-based solvers, especially large language model (LLM) based methods, in object search under the same computational constraints and perception systems. Real-world tests in office environments confirm the practical applicability of our approach. Project page: https://sites.google.com/view/gnpfkct.

\corrauth
Yongbo Chen, School of Automation and Intelligent Sensing, Shanghai Jiao Tong University, Shanghai, 200240, Pe
